title: Hashing
description: The mechanism behind unordered_map/unordered_set's speed, formalized — hash functions, collisions, and the classic Two Sum problem.
objectives: Understand what a hash function actually does
Understand hash collisions and how they're handled
Understand why hashing gives average O(1) operations
Solve the classic Two Sum problem using hashing

sections.text: 1. Recap: We've Used the Result, Not the Mechanism
unordered_set and unordered_map both mentioned "using hashing internally" for average O(1) performance, without explaining exactly how. Let's open that up.
A hash function converts a key into a number (a hash code), used to determine roughly where that key's data should be stored, so it can later be found again almost directly.

Instead of scanning through every stored key to find a match (like set's tree search, or a plain linear scan), hashing jumps almost straight to the right neighborhood — the specific bucket — cutting out most of the searching entirely.
A collision happens when two different keys hash to the same bucket — handled internally (commonly by storing a small list of entries per bucket) so both keys can still coexist correctly.

Collisions are normal and expected, not a bug — a good hash function just tries to keep them rare. When a bucket ends up with too many entries (many collisions), operations on that bucket degrade toward O(n) in the worst case, which is why unordered_map's guarantee is average O(1), not guaranteed O(1).

With a reasonable hash function and few collisions, computing a key's hash and jumping to its bucket takes constant time, regardless of how many total keys are stored — this is fundamentally different from set's O(log n) tree search, which genuinely gets slower as more elements are added.

sections.title: Why this beats the naive O(n²) nested-loop approach
points: For each number, we ask: 'have I already seen its complement (target - number)?' — an O(1) hash lookup instead of an O(n) inner scan
seen[complement] check + insertion, both O(1) average, replace what would otherwise be a full nested loop
Overall: O(n) time, O(n) space — trading space for a dramatic time improvement, another time-space tradeoff

sections.title: A library's call-number system
sections.text: A library doesn't search shelf by shelf for a book — a call number (derived from the book's category) sends you almost directly to the right shelf section. A hash function does the same for data: convert the key into a number that points nearly straight to where it's stored, instead of searching everything.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming hashing guarantees O(1) in every single case — it's average-case O(1); a poor hash function or adversarial input can degrade performance.
Reaching for a nested loop out of habit when a single pass with a hash map ('have I seen this before?') solves it in O(n).
In Two Sum, checking seen[complement] after inserting the current number instead of before — that could incorrectly match a number with itself.
Forgetting hashing trades space for time — an unordered_map/unordered_set uses extra O(n) memory to achieve its speed.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does a hash function do?
questions.answer: Converts a key into a number used to determine which bucket that key's data should be stored in, allowing it to be found again almost directly rather than searched for.

questions.question: What is a hash collision, and is it a problem?
questions.answer: When two different keys hash to the same bucket — it's expected and handled internally (e.g. multiple entries per bucket), not an error, though too many collisions degrade performance.

questions.question: Why is unordered_map's complexity described as 'average' O(1) rather than guaranteed O(1)?
questions.answer: Because a bucket with many collisions can degrade lookup toward O(n) in the worst case; average performance assumes a reasonably even distribution across buckets.

questions.question: How does hashing solve Two Sum in O(n) time?
questions.answer: By storing each number's index in a hash map as it's processed, then checking in O(1) whether the current number's complement (target - number) has already been seen, replacing an O(n) inner search with an O(1) lookup.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A hash function converts a key into a number used to place it into a specific bucket, letting it be found again almost directly rather than searched for — this is the mechanism behind unordered_map and unordered_set's average O(1) operations. Collisions, where two keys hash to the same bucket, are expected and handled internally, though heavy collisions can degrade performance toward O(n) in the worst case. The classic application is Two Sum: instead of checking every pair with nested loops (O(n²)), a hash map tracks numbers already seen, letting each number's complement be checked in O(1), for an overall O(n) time solution at the cost of O(n) extra space.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Hashing
sections.text: Hash function = a library call number, sends you almost straight to the shelf. Collision = two books with the same call number, handled, not broken. Two Sum = 'have I seen the complement?' — O(1) lookup instead of O(n) scan.

sections.title: Two Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target. Exactly one solution exists.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's core example — the complement-lookup idea, unrestricted by sortedness (unlike Two Sum II).
For each number, the question is: 'have I already seen target - number?' — track every number seen so far, along with its index.
A hash map gives O(1) average lookup for 'have I seen this value,' turning an O(n²) nested-loop search into a single O(n) pass.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair with nested loops.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A hash map tracking value-to-index, checked for each number's complement in a single pass.

sections.title: Contains Duplicate
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice.
hints: The question is 'have I seen this value before?' asked for every element — this lesson's exact hashing pattern, just with a hash SET instead of a map (no need to remember an index, only whether it's been seen).
Insert each value into a hash set as you scan; if a value is already in the set before you insert it, that's a duplicate.
A set's O(1) average membership check replaces the need to compare every pair.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair with nested loops for a match.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A hash set tracking values seen so far — an O(1) average membership check catches a duplicate the moment it's found.

sections.title: Valid Anagram
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s.
hints: A hash map is the natural tool for tallying 'how many of each key have I seen' — here, the key is a character instead of a whole array value.
Increment a count for every character in s, decrement for every character in t — an anagram means every count returns to exactly zero.
This is the same hash-map-as-a-tally pattern as this lesson's Two Sum, just counting occurrences instead of tracking indices.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort both strings and compare — true anagrams become identical once sorted.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A hash map tallying character counts — increment for s, decrement for t, and check everything cancels to zero.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) for the hash map, k = distinct characters

sections.title: Intersection of Two Arrays
sections.problemStatement: Given two arrays nums1 and nums2, return their intersection — each element in the result must be unique, in any order.
hints: 'Is this value present in the OTHER array' is a membership question — exactly what a hash set answers in O(1) average.
Put every value of nums1 into a hash set first, so checking whether a nums2 value exists in nums1 becomes a fast lookup instead of a scan.
The result itself needs unique values only — a second hash set for the result naturally avoids duplicates.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every element in nums2, scan all of nums1 checking for a match.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Put nums1 into a hash set for O(1) membership checks, then scan nums2 once, collecting matches into a second set to keep them unique.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for the two sets

sections.title: Intersection of Two Arrays II
sections.problemStatement: Given nums1 and nums2, return their intersection, where each element appears as many times as it shows in BOTH arrays (duplicates preserved, up to the minimum count).
hints: Unlike Intersection of Two Arrays, duplicates matter here — a plain hash SET isn't enough since it can't represent 'this value twice.'
A hash MAP tallying counts from nums1 tracks not just whether a value exists, but how many are still available to match.
Scanning nums2, a value can be added to the result as long as its count in the map is still greater than 0 — decrement the count each time it's used.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every element in nums2, scan nums1 for an unused match, marking matched elements so they aren't reused.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A hash map tallying nums1's counts; scanning nums2, use up a count each time a match is found.
